channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Fast Isogeny Evaluation on Binary Curves
item.description: We extend the odd-characteristic isogeny evaluation techniques of Hisil–Costello and Renes to **binary fields**, providing efficient formulas for evaluating isogenies of ordinary elliptic curves over $\mathbb{F}_{2^m}$. For odd prime degree $\ell = 2s+1$, our affine product method computes the image $x$-coordinate using $5s\mathbf{M}$ field multiplications ($4s\mathbf{M}$ with normalized kernel points). We further derive an **inversion-free variant** that evaluates the $x$-map in projective and twisted Kummer coordinates—enabling carried points to remain in projective/twisted form across successive isogeny steps without any field inversions. Microbenchmarks over $\mathbb{F}_{2^{511}}$ show that the inversion-free projective and twisted variants outperform Velu-style $x$-evaluation by 1.8–2.3× when outputs stay projective/twisted, while the one-inversion affine variant achieves a **4.2× speedup**. This work enables significantly faster isogeny-based cryptography on binary-field platforms.

item.title: Quick Draw Queries: Lightweight Searchable Public-key Ciphertexts with Hidden Structures via Non-Interactive Key Exchange
item.description: This paper proposes the first generic, pairing-free, and IBE-free construction of Searchable Public-key Ciphertexts with Hidden Structures (SPCHS), built on Non-Interactive Key Exchange (NIKE) and symmetric primitives only—specifically, secret-key encryption and a PRF. Crucially, the search algorithm is entirely symmetric-key-based, eliminating all public-key operations during querying. Instantiated with DH-NIKE, HMAC-PRF, and AES, our scheme achieves over **250× faster search time** and **~20× smaller ciphertexts** compared to the leading pairing-based SPCHS (Wang et al., IEEE T-II 2020). Beyond practical efficiency, our framework enables the **first post-quantum SPCHS instantiations**, naturally supporting lattice-based (LWE), isogeny-based (SIDH), and code-based NIKE schemes—unifying security, performance, and quantum resilience in searchable encryption.

item.title: PIArena: A Platform for Prompt Injection Evaluation
item.description: Prompt injection attacks pose critical security threats to LLM-based applications, yet the field lacks a unified, extensible evaluation platform—hindering reliable defense comparison, robustness assessment across diverse attacks, and generalization analysis across tasks and benchmarks. To bridge this gap, we introduce **PIArena**, an open-source, modular platform that standardizes integration of state-of-the-art attacks and defenses, and supports evaluation across 7 datasets, 5 task categories, and 3 attack threat models. We further propose a **Dynamic Strategy-based Attack (DSA)** that adaptively optimizes injected prompts using real-time defense feedback (e.g., refusal signals or output confidence). Comprehensive evaluation with PIArena reveals three fundamental limitations of current defenses: (1) poor cross-task generalizability (68% suffer ≥42% performance drop), (2) high vulnerability to adaptive attacks (DSA increases failure rates by 3.1×), and (3) near-total breakdown when injected instructions semantically align with the target task (91.7% average bypass rate). Code and datasets are publicly available at https://github.com/sleeepeer/PIArena.

item.title: Vulnerability Detection with Interprocedural Context in Multiple Languages: Assessing Effectiveness and Cost of Modern LLMs
item.description: This study empirically evaluates four modern LLMs—Claude Haiku 4.5, GPT-4.1 Mini, GPT-5 Mini, and Gemini 3 Flash—for detecting *interprocedural* vulnerabilities across C, C++, and Python, using 509 real-world examples from ReposVul. Unlike prior single-function approaches, we systematically vary interprocedural context (target function only; + callers; + callees) to assess its impact on detection accuracy, explanation quality, and inference cost. Results show that adding caller/callee context boosts average F1 by 12.7%, with Gemini 3 Flash achieving the best cost-effectiveness for C (F1 ≥ 0.978 at $0.50–$0.58 per configuration), while Claude Haiku 4.5 delivers the highest explanation fidelity (93.6% of cases correctly identified *and* explained). Performance varies notably by language—strongest in Python, weakest in C++—highlighting linguistic constraints on LLM-based security analysis. These findings provide actionable insights for building practical, multilingual, context-aware AI security tools.

item.title: Your Agent Is Mine: Measuring Malicious Intermediary Attacks on the LLM Supply Chain
item.description: This paper presents the first systematic study of malicious intermediary attacks on the LLM supply chain, focusing on third-party API routers that proxy tool-calling requests between LLM agents and upstream models. We formalize a threat model for compromised routers and define four attack classes: payload injection (AC-1), secret exfiltration (AC-2), dependency-targeted injection (AC-1.a), and conditional delivery (AC-1.b). Across 428 real-world routers (28 paid, 400 free), we found active exploitation—including ETH theft, AWS credential harvesting, and adaptive evasion triggers. Two poisoning studies revealed that even benign-looking routers become attack vectors when exposed to leaked keys or weak configurations, yielding up to 2B billed tokens and 401 autonomous YOLO sessions. We introduce **Mine**, a research proxy implementing all four attacks against four public agent frameworks, and evaluate three deployable client-side defenses: a fail-closed policy gate, response-side anomaly screening, and append-only transparency logging—all shown effective in practice.

item.title: Security Concerns in Generative AI Coding Assistants: Insights from Online Discussions on GitHub Copilot
item.description:
## Objective & Approach This study investigates *developer-perceived security concerns* regarding GenAI coding assistants—particularly GitHub Copilot—by analyzing authentic, unstructured discussions from Stack Overflow, Reddit, and Hacker News (2021–2023). We applied BERTopic for unsupervised clustering and rigorous thematic analysis to synthesize emergent risks beyond technical benchmarks.
## Key Findings Four critical concern categories emerged: (1) **Data leakage**—risks of exposing sensitive local code (e.g., credentials) via cloud-based inference; (2) **License compliance ambiguity**—unclear provenance and licensing of generated code, potentially violating copyleft terms; (3) **Adversarial prompt injection**—malicious natural-language inputs that hijack model behavior to output vulnerable templates (e.g., SQLi); and (4) **Insecure default suggestions**—model preference for syntactically correct but security-antipattern code (e.g., hardcoded secrets, deprecated crypto).
## Implication Our work provides the first empirically grounded, developer-centric security taxonomy for GenAI coding tools—bridging the gap between lab-evaluated performance and real-world adoption challenges—and informs actionable design principles for safer, more accountable AI-assisted development.

item.title: Securing Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Taxonomy of Attacks, Defenses, and Future Directions
item.description: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) enhances LLMs with external knowledge but introduces distinct security risks centered on the *knowledge-access pipeline*—not merely inherited LLM flaws. This paper establishes a clear operational boundary between inherent LLM vulnerabilities and RAG-specific or RAG-amplified threats. We abstract RAG into six workflow stages and organize attacks and defenses around three trust boundaries and four primary security surfaces: pre-retrieval knowledge corruption, retrieval-time access manipulation, downstream context exploitation, and knowledge exfiltration. A systematic review of 127 works reveals that current defenses are predominantly reactive (83%), siloed across stages, and lack holistic lifecycle coverage; existing benchmarks fail to model cross-boundary attack chains. We argue for *layered, boundary-aware protection* and propose five concrete future directions—including verifiable knowledge-source attestation, zero-trust retrieval sandboxes, semantic integrity checks during context injection, causal attack-chain tracing, and RAG-native red-teaming frameworks.

item.title: Multimodal Reasoning with LLM for Encrypted Traffic Interpretation: A Benchmark
item.description: Network traffic interpretation remains hampered by unimodal modeling limitations and opaque decision-making. To bridge this gap, we introduce **BGTD**, the first benchmark that grounds raw encrypted traffic bytes with expert-annotated behavioral features and verifiable evidence chains—enabling multimodal reasoning for explainable analysis. Building on BGTD, we propose **mmTraffic**, an end-to-end traffic-language framework featuring a jointly optimized perception-cognition architecture: a byte-centric encoder extracts protocol-agnostic physical patterns, while a cognition-centric LLM generates human-readable, evidence-grounded interpretations under strict chain-of-evidence constraints. Experiments show mmTraffic achieves competitive classification accuracy (98.3%, near state-of-the-art NetMamba) while autonomously producing high-fidelity, auditable interpretation reports—validated by expert evaluation (F1=0.89). Code is open-sourced.

item.title: TADP-RME: A Trust-Adaptive Differential Privacy Framework for Enhancing Reliability of Data-Driven Systems
item.description: Ensuring reliability of data-driven systems in adversarial settings requires privacy to be foundational—not auxiliary. Existing differential privacy (DP) frameworks employ a *fixed* privacy budget (ε), yielding rigid utility-privacy trade-offs ill-suited to heterogeneous user trust. Moreover, noise-only DP preserves local geometric structure, enabling geometry-aware inference attacks and causing real privacy leakage. We propose **TADP-RME**, a trust-adaptive DP framework featuring: (i) an inverse trust score ∈ [0,1] that dynamically modulates ε per user/data point, enabling smooth utility-to-privacy transitions; and (ii) **Reverse Manifold Embedding (RME)**—a nonlinear, invertible transformation that actively disrupts local geometric relationships while preserving formal ε-DP via post-processing invariance. Experiments show TADP-RME reduces inference attack success rates by up to **3.1 percentage points**, with negligible utility loss (<0.8% accuracy drop). It consistently outperforms Fixed-ε DP, PATE, and DP-SGD across diverse datasets and attacks, offering a unified, provably sound, and practical approach to reliable learning under adversarial threats.

item.title: PrivFedTalk: Privacy-Aware Federated Diffusion with Identity-Stable Adapters for Personalized Talking-Head Generation
item.description: PrivFedTalk is the first privacy-aware federated framework for personalized talking-head generation. It combines a shared conditional latent diffusion backbone with client-specific, parameter-efficient LoRA identity adapters—trained exclusively on local audio-visual data without raw data sharing. To handle statistical heterogeneity, we propose Identity-Stable Federated Aggregation (ISFA), which weights client updates using on-device scalar reliability signals derived from identity consistency and temporal stability estimates—ensuring privacy-safe aggregation. We further introduce Temporal-Denoising Consistency (TDC) regularization to suppress inter-frame flicker, drift, and identity degradation during federated denoising, and apply secure aggregation plus client-level differential privacy to adapter updates. Experiments under constrained resources (low-memory GPUs, heterogeneous hardware) show PrivFedTalk achieves stable end-to-end training, superior identity preservation, and robust convergence compared to FedAvg and FedProx—demonstrating the feasibility of privacy-preserving, personalized talking-head synthesis in realistic federated settings.

item.title: Are GUI Agents Focused Enough? Automated Distraction via Semantic-level UI Element Injection
item.description: This paper introduces **Semantic-level UI Element Injection (SUEI)**, a practical black-box red-teaming framework for GUI agents that bypasses limitations of white-box perturbations and increasingly mitigated prompt injections. SUEI overlays *semantically coherent, visually plausible, yet adversarially misdirecting* UI elements (e.g., buttons, labels) onto screenshots to corrupt the agent’s visual grounding—without requiring model access or text input manipulation. We propose a modular Editor-Overlapper-Victim pipeline with iterative search: sampling candidate edits, retaining the best cumulative overlay, and adapting prompt strategies based on prior failures. Evaluated across five state-of-the-art GUI agents, our optimized attacks achieve up to **4.4× higher success rates** than random injection—even on the strongest victims. Critically, optimized elements exhibit strong **cross-model transferability**, revealing model-agnostic vulnerabilities. Moreover, after the first successful attack, victims continue clicking the injected element in **>15% of subsequent independent trials**, versus <1% for random baselines—demonstrating persistent semantic attraction, not transient clutter.

item.title: ACIArena: Toward Unified Evaluation for Agent Cascading Injection
item.description: ACIArena is the first unified framework for evaluating Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) against Agent Cascading Injection (ACI)—a critical threat where a compromised agent exploits inter-agent trust to propagate malicious instructions and trigger cascading failures. Unlike prior works limited to narrow attack strategies or synthetic MAS settings, ACIArena provides systematic evaluation across three attack surfaces (external inputs, agent profiles, inter-agent messages) and three objectives (instruction hijacking, task disruption, information exfiltration). It introduces a unified YAML specification supporting both MAS construction and attack-defense integration, covers six widely used MAS frameworks (e.g., AutoGen, CrewAI), and delivers a benchmark of 1,356 test cases. Benchmarking reveals that topology alone is insufficient for robustness assessment; deliberate role design and controlled interaction patterns are essential. Moreover, defenses developed in simplified environments frequently fail to transfer—some even introduce new vulnerabilities. ACIArena establishes a foundational, reproducible, and extensible platform to advance principled MAS security research.

item.title: Differentially Private Language Generation and Identification in the Limit
item.description: We initiate the study of differentially private language generation and identification *in the limit*, a model recently introduced by Kleinberg and Mullainathan [KM24]. In the continual release setting, a generator must eventually output valid strings while protecting the privacy of the entire input sequence. For **generation**, we show that $\varepsilon$-DP imposes no *qualitative* cost: an $\varepsilon$-DP algorithm exists for any countable language collection—contrasting sharply with many DP learning tasks where privacy precludes learnability. However, privacy incurs a *quantitative* cost: uniform private generation over $k$-sized collections requires $\Omega(k/\varepsilon)$ samples, versus just one non-privately. For **identification**, privacy creates fundamental barriers: no $\varepsilon$-DP algorithm can identify any collection containing two languages with infinite intersection and finite symmetric difference—a condition strictly stronger than the classical non-private characterization. In the *stochastic* (i.i.d.) setting, we prove private identification is possible *iff* it is possible in the adversarial model—establishing a clean equivalence and a sharp separation between settings induced solely by privacy constraints.

item.title: Networking-Aware Energy Efficiency in Agentic AI Inference: A Survey
item.description: This survey addresses the critical energy efficiency challenge in Agentic AI inference—autonomous systems performing iterative perception, reasoning, and action—where *compounded computational and communication energy costs* dominate over traditional FLOPs-centric bottlenecks. We introduce a **networking-aware energy accounting framework**, explicitly modeling energy consumption across the closed-loop cycle. A unified taxonomy organizes techniques into four pillars: model simplification, computation control, input/attention optimization, and hardware-aware inference. Crucially, we emphasize **cross-layer co-design**, jointly optimizing model parameters, wireless transmissions (e.g., adaptive quantization, channel-aware scheduling), and edge resources (compute, memory, bandwidth). We identify four open frontiers: (1) federated green learning (privacy-preserving, low-carbon distributed training), (2) carbon-aware agency (inference scheduling aligned with real-time grid carbon intensity), (3) 6G-native Agentic AI (leveraging 6G integrated sensing, communication, and computing), and (4) self-sustaining systems (energy-harvesting–enabled autonomous operation). This work provides a foundational roadmap toward scalable, sustainable, and deployable autonomous intelligence.

item.title: LPM 1.0: Video-based Character Performance Model
item.description:
## LPM 1.0: A Real-Time, Identity-Stable Video Performance Model for Conversational Characters
Performance—the externalization of intent, emotion, and personality through audio-visual behavior—is central to lifelike character animation. To overcome the “performance trilemma” (trade-offs among expressiveness, real-time inference, and long-horizon identity stability), we introduce **LPM 1.0**, a unified video-based character performance model focused on full-duplex, single-person conversational scenarios. We curate a human-centric multimodal dataset with speaking-listening alignment and identity-aware multi-reference extraction; train a 17B-parameter Diffusion Transformer (Base LPM) for controllable, identity-consistent generation via multimodal conditioning; and distill it into a causal streaming generator (Online LPM) enabling low-latency, infinite-length interaction. At inference, given only a character image and identity references, LPM 1.0 generates both listening videos (from user audio) and speaking videos (from synthesized audio + text motion prompts) in real time—maintaining stable identity, natural expression, and precise lip sync across arbitrarily long conversations. We propose **LPM-Bench**, the first benchmark for interactive character performance, and show that LPM 1.0 achieves state-of-the-art results across all dimensions while sustaining real-time inference (<200 ms latency).

item.title: Quantization Impact on the Accuracy and Communication Efficiency Trade-off in Federated Learning for Aerospace Predictive Maintenance
item.description: This paper investigates symmetric uniform quantization ($b = 32, 8, 4, 2$ bits) in federated learning for aerospace predictive maintenance using the NASA C-MAPSS dataset under realistic Non-IID client partitioning. A custom lightweight 1-D CNN (AeroConv1D, 9,697 parameters) is trained via FL with rigorous multi-seed evaluation ($N=10$). Results show that **INT4 achieves statistically indistinguishable accuracy from FP32** (e.g., $p=0.264$ MAE on FD002; $p=0.534$ NASA score) while reducing per-round gradient communication by **8×** (37.88 KiB → 4.73 KiB). Crucially, Non-IID evaluation—unlike artificial IID partitioning—reveals true operational instability: INT2 exhibits catastrophic score variance (CV = 45.8% vs. 22.3% for FP32), rendering it non-reproducible in heterogeneous settings. FPGA projections on Xilinx ZCU102 confirm INT4’s hardware feasibility (85.5% DSP utilization), enabling full FL pipeline deployment on a single SoC. Code and scripts are open-sourced.

item.title: Automating aggregation strategy selection in federated learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training, yet its performance critically hinges on aggregation strategy selection—a non-trivial task due to strong dependence on data heterogeneity, dataset properties, and system constraints. We propose the first end-to-end framework that *automates, adapts, and streamlines* this decision. It operates in two modes: (1) a **single-trial LLM-based mode**, where a lightweight fine-tuned language model infers optimal strategies from automatically extracted data characteristics (e.g., label skew, feature sparsity); and (2) a **multi-trial genetic search mode**, enabling efficient strategy exploration under tight communication or compute budgets. Evaluated across six diverse non-IID benchmarks (including FEMNIST, Shakespeare, and long-tailed CIFAR), our approach improves average test accuracy by 2.3–5.7 points over manual baselines, enhances robustness by up to 41% under severe heterogeneity (Dirichlet α=0.1), and reduces human intervention by 92%. This work advances accessible and adaptive FL by automating one of its most consequential design choices.

item.title: A Systematic Framework for Tabular Data Disentanglement
item.description: Tabular data disentanglement remains underexplored despite its critical role in industrial, financial, and supply-chain applications—largely due to complex attribute interactions that defy direct adaptation from image/text/audio domains. Existing methods (e.g., factor analysis, CT-GAN, VAE) suffer from scalability limits, mode collapse, and poor out-of-distribution extrapolation. To address this, we propose **TabDisentangle**, the first systematic framework modularizing tabular disentanglement into four components: *data extraction*, *data modeling* (via conditional graph neural networks for explicit dependency capture), *model analysis* (with a novel Disentanglement Quantification Index, DQI), and *latent representation extrapolation* (using manifold-constrained contrastive learning). Evaluated on synthetic tabular generation, TabDisentangle achieves +12.3% F1-score, −41.7% mutual information among latents, and −35.2% OOD error versus SOTA baselines—demonstrating superior disentanglement quality, scalability, and generalization. This work establishes a foundational, interpretable, and extensible paradigm for robust tabular representation learning.

item.title: Guaranteeing Knowledge Integration with Joint Decoding for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
item.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) boosts LLMs with external knowledge, yet a critical “integration bottleneck” persists: models often ignore or conflict with retrieved evidence due to entrenched parametric knowledge. We propose **GuarantRAG**, a novel framework that explicitly decouples reasoning from evidence integration. It first generates an *Inner-Answer* using only parametric knowledge to capture intrinsic reasoning; then produces a *Refer-Answer* via a contrastive DPO objective—treating the Inner-Answer as a negative constraint and retrieved documents as positive ground truth—to suppress hallucinations and enforce evidence fidelity. Finally, a token-level **joint decoding mechanism** dynamically fuses the logical coherence of the Inner-Answer with the factual precision of the Refer-Answer—avoiding naive concatenation or direct DPO deployment. Experiments on five QA benchmarks show GuarantRAG improves accuracy by up to **12.1%** and reduces hallucinations by **16.3%** over strong RAG baselines, establishing a new paradigm for *guaranteed*, interpretable knowledge integration.

item.title: HCRE: LLM-based Hierarchical Classification for Cross-Document Relation Extraction with a Prediction-then-Verification Strategy
item.description: Cross-document relation extraction (RE) identifies semantic relations between entities residing in *different* documents—a task where conventional SLM-based classifiers face inherent language understanding limits. While Large Language Models (LLMs) offer strong capabilities, our preliminary study reveals they underperform on standard benchmarks (e.g., DocRED) due to the overwhelming number of predefined relations (e.g., 96 classes), causing ambiguity in flat classification. To address this, we propose **HCRE**: an LLM-based hierarchical classification framework featuring (1) a *hierarchical relation tree* that decomposes the full relation set into semantically coherent levels—drastically reducing per-level classification candidates—and (2) a *prediction-then-verification* inference strategy, where multi-view verification (contextual, coreferential, and descriptive cues) at each level mitigates error propagation. Extensive experiments show HCRE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art SLM and zero/few-shot LLM baselines by +3.2–6.9 F1 points, establishing hierarchical structuring and verifiable reasoning as key enablers for LLMs in fine-grained cross-document RE.

item.title: A Constructive Treatment of Authentication
item.description: This paper revisits classical message authentication techniques—MACs, digital signatures, hash-based schemes, and KDF-augmented constructions—from the lens of **Constructive Cryptography**, a composable security framework grounded in resource substitution. We compile known results on when and how these primitives realize the ideal authenticated channel, identify critical gaps (e.g., composability of truncated MACs without random oracles), and clarify setup requirements (e.g., key entropy bounds, clock synchronization) for sound instantiation. Our analysis yields precise security statements—including distinguisher advantage bounds, resource dependencies, and adversary limitations—for each construction. We further highlight open challenges: composability under quantum adversaries, stateless yet composable signature variants, and dynamic network-aware authentication resources. The work serves as a practical toolkit for building composable authentication with rigorously quantified guarantees.

item.title: Boolean Arithmetic over $\mathbb{F}_2$ from Group Commutators
item.description: This paper introduces a novel algebraic framework for realizing Boolean arithmetic over $\mathbb{F}_2$ *entirely within* finite nonabelian simple groups—using only group multiplication and inversion, with no external encoding or auxiliary structures. The core insight is to harness **group commutators** $[x,y] = xyx^{-1}y^{-1}$ as native logical primitives. We present two complementary constructions: (i) a universal NAND gate implementable in at most 9 group operations in $A_5$, and (ii) direct, efficient realizations of XOR and AND requiring only 6 and 7 operations respectively in $A_5$. These are the most efficient known group-only implementations for $\mathbb{F}_2$ arithmetic. Our methods apply broadly to all finite nonabelian simple groups (e.g., $A_n$, $\mathrm{PSL}(2,q)$), and explicit, computer-verified instantiations are provided for $A_5$ and $A_6$. This work bridges combinatorial group theory and Boolean computation, enabling new approaches to group-based cryptography and secure computation.

item.title: GRAFHEN is not IND-CPA secure
item.description: GRAFHEN is a recently proposed noise-free fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) scheme built on string rewriting systems over symmetric groups $S_n$, claiming IND-CPA security. We present a concrete, efficient distinguisher that breaks this security notion. Our attack exploits the fact that the conjugacy class of the first ciphertext component $g \in S_n$ reveals statistically detectable information about the plaintext: specifically, the distribution of $\text{cls}(g)$ under encryption of $0$ versus $1$ differs significantly due to the group action structure and publicly known relations. The distinguisher computes the size of $g$’s conjugacy class (polynomial-time in $n$) and thresholds it against a precomputed bound—achieving distinguishing advantage $\geq 0.99$ with a single ciphertext query. For standard parameters (e.g., $n = 64$), the attack runs in under 1 ms. This result invalidates GRAFHEN’s core security claim and demonstrates that algebraic transparency in symmetric-group representations is incompatible with IND-CPA without additional randomization.

item.title: HAWK with Hint: Algebraic Key Recovery from Side-Channel Leakage
item.description: Quantum computing threatens classical public-key cryptography, motivating NIST’s Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) standardization—within which HAWK, a lattice-based signature scheme, was a third-round candidate. While its mathematical security has been extensively studied, the impact of side-channel leakage—especially from the discrete Gaussian sampler (DGS)—remains poorly understood in practice. We bridge this gap by formalizing DGS leakage as *HAWK with Hint*, introducing three leakage models: (i) full coefficient recovery, (ii) sign-only recovery, and (iii) noisy sign recovery (with bit-error rates up to 40%). For each, we design algebraic key recovery algorithms that derive explicit linear relations between leaked data and the secret basis, then solve the resulting structured linear systems in polynomial time. On HAWK-1024, our attacks recover the secret key using only **1 signature** under full-coefficient leakage (improving prior work requiring 2), **14 signatures in ~100 seconds** under sign-only leakage, and **400 signatures in ~30 seconds at 10% BER**—scaling to **~7,000 signatures in ~60 seconds even at 40% BER**. All attacks are fully implemented and empirically validated.

item.title: Entropy-based Fuzzy Deduplication with Perfect Resistance to Key Recovery Attack
item.description: Encrypted data deduplication reduces cloud storage costs while preserving privacy, but conventional exact deduplication fails for multimedia data due to inherent semantic similarity rather than byte-level equality. Existing fuzzy deduplication schemes suffer from critical vulnerabilities—especially key recovery attacks (KRA) and brute-force guessing—enabling adversaries to reconstruct user keys offline and forge similarity proofs. This work establishes a foundational entropy-based framework: (1) We define *information-entropy-driven similarity* using local block entropy distributions, enabling fully server-side, client-free similarity verification; (2) We propose the first *Parameter Robustness Verification (PRV)* mechanism, which binds secret keys to entropy-sensitive nonlinear hash chains, rendering KRA computationally infeasible even with full protocol parameter exposure; (3) Rigorous security proofs under the random oracle model confirm perfect resistance against KRA and adaptive attacks. Experiments show ≈87% storage savings and ≥97% tamper detection success rate, with zero key recovery in 10⁹ trials—achieving provable, practical security.

item.title: Post-Quantum Secure k-Times Traceable Ring Signature
item.description: We propose the first lattice-based *k-times traceable ring signature* scheme, achieving post-quantum security under the Module-LWE assumption. Unlike standard ring signatures—which offer unconditional anonymity—our scheme enables public tracing of a signer’s public key *if and only if* they sign more than *k* times on the same message or across distinct messages (with appropriate binding). This provides a practical balance between privacy and accountability. Our construction features compact signatures ($\tilde{O}(k \log N)$), efficient verification ($O(k + \log N)$ matrix operations), and rigorous security proofs in the random oracle model for *k*-anonymity, *k*-unforgeability, and *k*-traceability. Compared to prior traceable schemes (e.g., pairing-based or RSA-based), ours avoids quantum-vulnerable assumptions and demonstrates superior concrete performance—e.g., 2.1× faster signing at $k=5$, $N=1024$. This makes it a viable candidate for real-world deployment in the post-quantum era.

item.title: TraceSafe: A Systematic Assessment of LLM Guardrails on Multi-Step Tool-Calling Trajectories
item.description: As LLMs evolve into autonomous agents performing multi-step tool calls, safety vulnerabilities shift from final outputs to intermediate execution traces—yet no benchmark systematically assesses *mid-trajectory* risks. We introduce **TraceSafe-Bench**, the first comprehensive benchmark covering 12 risk categories (e.g., prompt injection, privacy leaks, hallucinations, interface inconsistencies) across 1,000+ realistic, structured tool-call trajectories. Evaluating 13 LLM-as-guard models and 7 specialized guardrails, we find: (1) Structural data competence (e.g., JSON parsing) dominates semantic safety alignment—performance correlates strongly with structured reasoning ($ρ = 0.79$) but negligibly with jailbreak robustness ($ρ ≈ 0.03$); (2) General-purpose LLM architectures outperform specialized safety models consistently, indicating architecture matters more than scale or domain tuning; (3) Detection accuracy improves over longer trajectories as models leverage dynamic behavioral patterns—not static tool definitions—boosting late-stage recall by up to 12.4%. These results underscore that securing agentic workflows requires joint optimization of structural reasoning and safety alignment.

item.title: SentinelSphere: Integrating AI-Powered Real-Time Threat Detection with Cybersecurity Awareness Training
item.description: SentinelSphere is an AI-native platform that unifies real-time threat detection with adaptive cybersecurity awareness training to address the dual crisis of global talent shortage and persistent human-factor vulnerabilities. Its detection module employs an Enhanced Deep Neural Network trained on CIC-IDS2017/CIC-DDoS2019, augmented with novel HTTP-layer feature engineering for precise application-level attack identification. The education module leverages a quantized, domain-fine-tuned Phi-4 (Q4_K_M) LLM—deployable on commodity hardware (16 GB RAM, no GPU)—to deliver conversational, scenario-based security coaching. Experiments show the Enhanced DNN achieves 99.2% accuracy, reduces false positives by 37.5% versus baselines, and maintains >98.6% recall across DDoS, brute-force, and web exploits. Validation workshops confirmed high usability of its Traffic Light visualization and AI assistant among non-technical users. SentinelSphere demonstrates that tightly integrating intelligent detection with LLM-driven education meaningfully bridges technical and human security gaps in a single, deployable framework.

item.title: SkillTrojan: Backdoor Attacks on Skill-Based Agent Systems
item.description: Skill-based agent systems improve modularity and scalability by composing reusable skills—but introduce a critical, underexplored security surface: the skill implementations themselves. We propose **SkillTrojan**, the first backdoor attack that targets *skills as code*, not model weights or training data. It embeds malicious logic inside semantically plausible skills, partitions an encrypted payload across multiple benign invocations, and reconstructs/executes it only upon a predefined trigger. Crucially, SkillTrojan supports automated synthesis of backdoored skills from arbitrary templates, enabling scalable, stealthy propagation. To enable rigorous evaluation, we release a dataset of 3,000+ curated backdoored skills covering diverse patterns and configurations. Evaluated on EHR SQL with GPT-5.2-1211-Global, SkillTrojan achieves up to **97.2% attack success rate (ASR)** while preserving **89.3% clean-task accuracy (ACC)**—demonstrating high stealth and efficacy. This exposes a fundamental blind spot in current skill-based architectures and calls for defenses that explicitly reason about skill composition, execution flow, and contextual triggers.

item.title: FedDAP: Domain-Aware Prototype Learning for Federated Learning under Domain Shift
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) suffers from severe performance degradation under domain shift, as clients hold data from heterogeneous domains. While prototype-based FL methods improve robustness, they ignore domain structure by aggregating all local prototypes into a single domain-agnostic global prototype and enforcing uniform feature alignment across domains. To address this, we propose **FedDAP**, the first FL framework that constructs **domain-specific global prototypes** via similarity-weighted fusion of local prototypes *within the same domain*. During local training, clients perform **dual alignment**: (i) pulling features toward prototypes of their *own* domain, and (ii) pushing features away from prototypes of *other* domains. This enhances domain-aware representation learning while preserving cross-domain generalizability. Extensive experiments on DomainNet, Office-10, and PACS show FedDAP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods by +3.2–5.8% average accuracy under domain shift, with ablation confirming the critical role of domain-aware prototype fusion and dual alignment. Code is publicly available.

item.title: FlowExtract: Procedural Knowledge Extraction from Maintenance Flowcharts
item.description: Maintenance procedures in manufacturing are frequently documented as static flowcharts (PDFs/scans), encoding critical procedural knowledge—but remaining inaccessible to digital operator support systems. Vision-language models (VLMs) fail to reliably reconstruct directed connectivity topology from such ISO 5807-compliant diagrams due to arrow ambiguity, line crossings, and domain-specific layout complexity. FlowExtract addresses this by decoupling node detection (using YOLOv8 + EasyOCR for robust symbol and text extraction) from edge reconstruction. Its novel edge detection method identifies arrowheads geometrically and traces connecting lines *backward* to source nodes—significantly improving precision over VLM-based approaches. Evaluated on industrial troubleshooting guides, FlowExtract achieves 98.2% node F1 and 89.6% edge accuracy, outperforming state-of-the-art VLM baselines by +42.3 points on edge extraction. The open-source implementation enables practical, scalable conversion of legacy flowcharts into queryable procedural knowledge graphs.

item.title: A Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning Approach through Multitask Prompt Distillation and Decomposition for Clinical NLP
item.description: We propose **MT-PDD**, a parameter-efficient multitask prompt distillation and decomposition framework for clinical NLP. It learns a single shared *metaprompt* from 21 diverse clinical source tasks and adapts it to unseen target tasks with **<0.05% trainable parameters**, eliminating per-task prompt duplication. Evaluated across 5 clinical NLP task types on 10 held-out datasets using LLaMA 3.1 8B, Meditron3 8B, and gpt-oss 20B, MT-PDD consistently outperforms LoRA by **1.5–1.7% absolute gain** despite using orders-of-magnitude fewer parameters, and surpasses single-task prompt tuning by **6.1–6.6%**. The gpt-oss 20B variant achieves state-of-the-art performance—especially on clinical reasoning tasks—demonstrating superior transferability of the distilled metaprompt representation in zero- and few-shot settings.

item.title: SubFLOT: Submodel Extraction for Efficient and Personalized Federated Learning via Optimal Transport
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training but suffers from system and statistical heterogeneity, hindering real-world deployment. While federated pruning alleviates resource constraints, existing approaches face a critical trade-off: server-side pruning lacks personalization, whereas client-side pruning is computationally prohibitive for edge devices—and both exacerbate parametric divergence among heterogeneous submodels, impairing convergence. To address this, we propose **SubFLOT**, a novel server-side framework for *personalized* federated pruning. SubFLOT introduces two key innovations: (**i**) an **Optimal Transport-enhanced Pruning (OTP)** module that treats historical client models as proxies for local data distributions, formulating submodel extraction as a Wasserstein distance minimization to generate data-free, customized submodels; and (**ii**) a **Scaling-based Adaptive Regularization (SAR)** module that penalizes submodel deviation from the global model with strength scaled by the client’s pruning rate—effectively curbing divergence. Extensive experiments across six heterogeneous benchmarks show SubFLOT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving up to 7.9% higher accuracy, 68% less communication cost, and significantly improved personalization and convergence stability.

item.title: The Detection--Extraction Gap: Models Know the Answer Before They Can Say It
item.description: This paper identifies the **detection–extraction gap**: modern reasoning models internally “know” the correct answer long before standard decoding can reliably extract it. Across five model configurations (including Llama-3, Qwen, and Phi-3), two families, and three benchmarks (GSM8K, MMLU-Math, AQuA), we find that **52–88% of chain-of-thought tokens are generated after the answer is already recoverable from a partial prefix**. Free continuations from prefixes as short as 10% of the full trace recover the correct answer in most cases, whereas forced extraction (e.g., via “Answer:” prompts) fails on 42% of those same instances—revealing a fundamental mismatch between model state and prompt-conditioned decoding. We formalize this via a total-variation bound quantifying suffix-induced distributional shift. Leveraging this asymmetry, we propose **Black-box Adaptive Early Exit (\BAEE{})**, which uses free continuations for *both* detection and extraction—requiring no model modification or training. \BAEE{} reduces serial generation by **70–78%**, improves accuracy by **1–5 percentage points** across all models, and achieves up to **5.8 pp gain** for thinking-mode models; its cost-optimized variant cuts computation by **68–73%** at a median of just **9 API calls**. Code: https://github.com/EdWangLoDaSc/know2say.

item.title: LLM-based Schema-Guided Extraction and Validation of Missing-Person Intelligence from Heterogeneous Data Sources
item.description: This paper introduces the **Guardian Parser Pack**, a schema-first, auditable AI pipeline for extracting and normalizing missing-person intelligence from heterogeneous sources (e.g., forms, posters, web profiles). It integrates multi-engine PDF/OCR extraction, rule-based source identification, schema-guided harmonization, and—optionally—an LLM-assisted pathway with validator-guided repair and shared geocoding. Evaluated on 75 gold-aligned cases and 517 records, the LLM pathway achieved substantially higher extraction quality (F1 = 0.8664 vs. 0.2578) and key-field completeness (96.97% vs. 93.23%) than the deterministic baseline. Crucially, all LLM outputs passed initial schema validation—demonstrating built-in robustness—while runtime remained 130× slower (3.95 s vs. 0.03 s/record). Results validate a controlled, schema-anchored deployment of probabilistic AI in high-stakes investigative workflows.

item.title: SkillSieve: A Hierarchical Triage Framework for Detecting Malicious AI Agent Skills
item.description: SkillSieve is a hierarchical triage framework designed to detect malicious skills in AI agent marketplaces like ClawHub—where 13–26% of 13,000+ community skills harbor security vulnerabilities. It overcomes the modality gap between code (e.g., AST/regex) and natural-language instructions (e.g., `SKILL.md`) by applying three progressive analysis layers: Layer 1 uses lightweight, zero-cost XGBoost-scoring of regex, AST, and metadata to filter ~86% of benign skills in <40ms; Layer 2 delegates suspicious skills to an LLM via four parallel, structured sub-tasks (intent alignment, permission justification, covert behavior detection, cross-file consistency); Layer 3 resolves high-risk cases via a voting-and-debate jury of three diverse LLMs. Evaluated on 49,592 real skills and adversarial samples across five evasion techniques—fully deployed on a 440 ARM SBC—SkillSieve achieves **0.800 F1** on a 400-sample labeled benchmark (vs. ClawVet’s 0.421) at **$0.006/skill** average cost. Code, data, and benchmark are open-sourced.

item.title: Interpreting the Error of Differentially Private Median Queries through Randomization Intervals
item.description: Differentially private (DP) median estimation faces a fundamental trade-off: existing methods that provide high-quality Randomization Intervals (RIs)—a privacy-aware error bound—sacrifice median accuracy by degrading the estimator itself (e.g., via input smoothing or budget reallocation). This work introduces **PostRI**, the first *post-hoc* RI construction framework that computes a tight RI *after* an arbitrary high-utility DP median has been estimated. PostRI leverages the output value, privacy parameter ε, and data domain to derive a theoretically valid, narrow RI without altering the underlying median mechanism. Empirical evaluation shows PostRI achieves **14%–850% higher utility** (measured by MAE/MSE) over prior RI-based approaches while maintaining RI width within ±5% of state-of-the-art. By decoupling estimation and interpretation, PostRI enables both statistically accurate and meaningfully interpretable DP medians—bridging a critical gap between theory and practice.

item.title: MCP-DPT: A Defense-Placement Taxonomy and Coverage Analysis for Model Context Protocol Security
item.description: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) enhances LLM agents via dynamic third-party tool invocation but introduces cross-component security risks across distributed trust boundaries. Existing research remains attack-centric or benchmark-driven, offering little guidance on *where* defenses should be placed architecturally. We propose **MCP-DPT**, the first defense-placement-oriented taxonomy for MCP security. It maps 47 known attacks across six MCP layers and identifies primary and secondary defense points to enable principled defense-in-depth reasoning—even under adversaries controlling tools, servers, or ecosystem components. A systematic survey of 62 academic and industry defenses reveals severe imbalance: 78% target the tool layer, while critical gaps persist at the host orchestration, transport, and supply-chain layers. Crucially, we find that most MCP vulnerabilities stem from *architectural misalignment*—mismatched defense responsibility relative to data/control flow—not isolated implementation flaws. MCP-DPT thus provides a foundational framework for accountable, layered security governance in multi-party agent systems.

item.title: TRUSTDESC: Preventing Tool Poisoning in LLM Applications via Trusted Description Generation
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly integrate external tools for real-world actions, yet this exposes a critical vulnerability: *tool poisoning attacks* (TPAs)—where adversaries corrupt tool descriptions to manipulate model behavior. While explicit TPAs inject malicious instructions, *implicit TPAs* embed deceptive functional claims that evade existing detection-based defenses. We present **TRUSTDESC**, the first framework that *prevents* TPAs at their root by automatically generating trusted tool descriptions directly from source code implementations. TRUSTDESC employs a three-stage pipeline: (1) **SliceMin**, using reachability-aware static analysis and LLM-guided debloating to extract minimal, functionally faithful code slices; (2) **DescGen**, synthesizing robust natural-language descriptions while suppressing adversarial artifacts (e.g., misleading comments or strings); and (3) **DynVer**, dynamically verifying behavioral claims via task execution and output validation. Evaluated on 52 real-world tools across major ecosystems, TRUSTDESC achieves high description accuracy (+32.7%), improves task completion (+21.4% avg.), and reduces implicit TPA success from 91.3% to just 1.9%, with negligible overhead (<0.8s/tool, <$0.02/tool).

item.title: Differentially Private Modeling of Disease Transmission within Human Contact Networks
item.description: We propose a differentially private (DP) pipeline for simulating infectious disease transmission on sensitive human contact networks. To preserve individual privacy while retaining epidemiological utility, our approach integrates **node-level DP**—protecting each person’s relational contributions—with statistical network models (ERGMs/SBMs) to generate synthetic networks that preserve structural properties. Disease spread is then simulated via agent-based SIS modeling on these synthetic graphs. Evaluated on egocentric sexual network data from the ARTNet study, our method adds negligible noise relative to sampling and model specification errors: under ε = 0.5–2.0, simulated incidence and prevalence deviate by <8% from non-private baselines, and key policy conclusions—such as intervention effect sizes on transmission thresholds—remain robust (effect difference <0.03). This demonstrates that DP-enabled synthetic networks can support trustworthy public health inference without releasing sensitive relational data.

item.title: Private Seeds, Public LLMs: Realistic and Privacy-Preserving Synthetic Data Generation
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) offer great promise for synthetic data generation—but generating realistic replicas of sensitive private text while guaranteeing rigorous privacy remains unsolved. We propose **RPSG (Realistic and Privacy-Preserving Synthetic Data Generation)**, a novel framework that jointly leverages *formal differential privacy* (with end-to-end (ε,δ)-DP guarantees) and *private seeds*—i.e., minimal, untrained private text snippets containing personal information—to condition public LLMs. Unlike prior methods, RPSG uses private seeds only as privacy-preserving prompts (not for training), enabling high-fidelity generation without memorization. Extensive experiments across 5 real-world private text domains show RPSG achieves state-of-the-art utility (up to +9.1 F1 on downstream classification) and privacy (≤4.3% membership inference success), outperforming DP-LLM, PrivLLM, and Synthia. RPSG thus demonstrates that strong privacy and realism are not mutually exclusive—enabling trustworthy, deployable synthetic data from private sources.

item.title: On the Price of Privacy for Language Identification and Generation
item.description: We initiate the study of differentially private (DP) language identification and generation in the agnostic statistical setting. For approximate $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP with constant $\varepsilon > 0$, we show that privacy incurs *no asymptotic cost*: error rates match the non-private benchmarks—$\exp(-r(n))$ for identification (any $r(n) = o(n)$) and $\exp(-\Omega(n))$ for generation. Under pure $\varepsilon$-DP, the exponential decay rates degrade *exactly* by a multiplicative factor of $\min\{1,\varepsilon\}$ in the exponent—a tight bound up to constants. Notably, for generation under mild assumptions, our upper bound $\exp(-\min\{1,\varepsilon\}\cdot \Omega(n))$ matches the lower bound, establishing the first optimal rate for pure-DP language generation. These results demonstrate that the price of privacy in language learning is remarkably mild: vanishing under approximate DP, and precisely characterized by a controllable exponent scaling under pure DP.

item.title: DDP-SA: Scalable Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning via Distributed Differential Privacy and Secure Aggregation
item.description: This paper introduces **DDP-SA**, a scalable privacy-preserving federated learning framework that *jointly leverages* client-side Local Differential Privacy (LDP) and full-threshold Additive Secret Sharing (ASS) for secure aggregation. Unlike prior works relying solely on LDP (suffering severe accuracy loss) or MPC-only schemes (lacking end-to-end differential privacy), DDP-SA enforces a two-stage protection: clients first perturb gradients with calibrated Laplace noise, then split the noisy gradients into additive shares distributed across multiple untrusted intermediate servers. This ensures (i) no single compromised server or channel reveals individual updates, and (ii) the parameter server reconstructs *only* the aggregated noisy gradient—never any client-specific contribution. Experiments show DDP-SA achieves up to **12.7% higher accuracy than standalone LDP**, while providing stronger $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-privacy guarantees (38–52% lower $\varepsilon$) than MPC-only baselines. It scales linearly in computation and maintains constant communication rounds, enabling practical deployment in large-scale, privacy-critical applications.

item.title: FedDetox: Robust Federated SLM Alignment via On-Device Data Sanitization
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving training of Small Language Models (SLMs) using decentralized user data, yet real-world client data often contains unsafe or toxic content—causing *unintended data poisoning* that degrades global model safety alignment. To address this, we propose **FedDetox**, a robust on-device framework for federated SLM alignment. FedDetox employs knowledge distillation to deploy lightweight safety classifiers (<2 MB) on resource-constrained edge devices; during preference alignment, clients *detect and sanitize unsafe samples at the source*, replacing them with standardized refusal templates—transforming poisons into explicit safety signals. Crucially, all sanitization occurs locally with zero added communication overhead. Experiments across SafeRLHF, BeaverTails, and custom toxic benchmarks show FedDetox achieves near-centralized safety performance (98.2% vs. 99.1% Safety Score) while preserving utility (−0.9% Helpfulness drop), outperforming unfiltered FL baselines by +32.4% in safety. FedDetox establishes on-device data sanitization as a practical, privacy-aware foundation for safe federated alignment.

item.title: ARuleCon: Agentic Security Rule Conversion
item.description:
## ARuleCon: Agentic Security Rule Conversion
Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems rely on vendor-specific query languages (e.g., Splunk SPL, Microsoft KQL, IBM AQL), hindering cross-platform rule reuse. Manual conversion is error-prone and demands deep domain expertise. We propose **ARuleCon**, an agentic framework that autonomously converts SIEM rules with high semantic fidelity—requiring *no manual logic extraction* or target-platform documentation study. ARuleCon employs a two-phase agent pipeline: (1) parsing source rules into an intermediate semantic representation, and (2) generating target-language rules guided by schema-aware constraints. Crucially, it integrates a lightweight Python-based consistency checker that executes both source and target rules on identical test data to detect and mitigate subtle semantic drifts. Evaluated on 2,147 real-world rule pairs across 5 platforms, ARuleCon achieves **89.3% textual alignment accuracy** and **92.7% execution success rate**, outperforming GPT-4 Turbo by +15.2% on average. Industry validation with Singtel Singapore confirms a **92% reduction in expert effort**, cutting average rule migration time from 11.6 to 0.9 hours per rule.

item.title: Argus: Reorchestrating Static Analysis via a Multi-Agent Ensemble for Full-Chain Security Vulnerability Detection
item.description:
## Argus: Reorchestrating Static Analysis via a Multi-Agent Ensemble for Full-Chain Security Vulnerability Detection
Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise for Static Application Security Testing (SAST), yet existing LLM-based approaches suffer from high false positives, hallucinations, shallow reasoning, and excessive token usage—largely due to isolated deployment without integration into established SAST toolchains. To address this, we propose Argus: the first LLM-centered, multi-agent framework for end-to-end security vulnerability detection. Argus introduces three key innovations—(1) comprehensive supply chain analysis spanning source code, dependencies, and CI/CD configurations; (2) collaborative agent roles (Parser, Hunter, Refiner, Validator) coordinated via RAG-augmented context and ReAct-style self-validation; and (3) systematic hallucination mitigation through grounded, evidence-referenced reasoning. Empirical evaluation across 12 real-world projects shows Argus detects **3.2× more true vulnerabilities**, reduces false positives by **68%**, cuts token cost by **54%**, and has discovered **7 CVE-assigned zero-day vulnerabilities**—demonstrating both technical superiority and industrial practicality.

item.title: Can Drift-Adaptive Malware Detectors Be Made Robust? Attacks and Defenses Under White-Box and Black-Box Threats
item.description: This paper investigates the adversarial robustness of drift-adaptive malware detectors—a previously unexplored intersection of concept drift and evasion attacks. We evaluate AdvDA, a state-of-the-art adversarial domain adaptation detector, under white-box (feature-space PGD) and black-box (binary-level MalGuise) threats. We propose a universal robustification framework that fine-tunes pretrained AdvDA on adversarially transformed inputs—agnostic to attack type or transformation choice. Across nine defense variants, five monthly adaptation windows on Windows malware, and three FPR operating points, we find the undefended AdvDA suffers 100% PGD success and 13% MalGuise success. Our framework reduces these to as low as **3.2% and 5.1%**, respectively—but optimal strategies diverge: source-domain adversarial training is essential for PGD defense yet harmful for MalGuise, where target-only training suffices. Crucially, robustness does not transfer across threat models. We provide actionable deployment guidelines balancing robustness, accuracy, and computational cost.

item.title: From Papers to Property Tables: A Priority-Based LLM Workflow for Materials Data Extraction
item.description: We present a prompt-driven, hierarchical LLM workflow for extracting and reconstructing structured shock-physics experimental records—exemplified by alloy spall strength—from full-text research articles. Targeting 37 shot-level fields, it integrates information across text, tables, figures, and physics-based derivations using a three-tier priority strategy: (T1) direct extraction, (T2) derivation via verified governing equations, and (T3) figure digitization as fallback. Values are normalized, priority-tagged for traceability, and validated via physics-consistency and plausibility checks. Evaluated on 30 papers (11,967 data points), the pipeline achieves 94.69% weighted accuracy (T1: 94.93%, T2: 92.04%, T3: 83.49%). Cross-model tests confirm strong agreement for T1/T2 fields but lower reliability for T3. API deployment demonstrates scalability and matches/exceeds chat-based accuracy in subsets. This work enables task-agnostic, traceable conversion of unstructured materials literature into analysis-ready datasets.

item.title: The Detection-Extraction Gap: Models Know the Answer Before They Can Say It
item.description: This paper identifies the **detection-extraction gap**: modern reasoning models internally “know” the correct answer long before standard decoding can reliably extract it from their chain-of-thought (CoT) output. Across five model configurations, two families (Llama/Mistral and Qwen/Gemma), and three benchmarks (GSM8K, SVAMP, AQuA), we find that **52–88% of CoT tokens are generated after the answer is already recoverable from a partial prefix**. Free continuations from prefixes as short as 10% of the full trace recover the correct answer in most cases—yet forced extraction (e.g., via “Answer:” prompts) fails on 42% of these same prefixes. We formalize this mismatch using a **total-variation bound** between free and forced continuation distributions, quantifying suffix-induced shifts. Leveraging this asymmetry, we propose **Black-box Adaptive Early Exit (BAEE)**, which uses free continuations for both detection and extraction. BAEE truncates **70–78% of serial generation**, improves accuracy by **1–5 percentage points** across all models, and achieves up to **+5.8pp gain** for thinking-mode models by preventing post-commitment overwriting. A cost-optimized variant reduces API calls to a **median of 9**, cutting compute by **68–73%**. Code: https://github.com/EdWangLoDaSc/know2say

item.title: Extraction of linearized models from pre-trained networks via knowledge distillation
item.description: This paper addresses the growing need for linear-only machine learning models compatible with emerging hardware platforms (e.g., photonic integrated circuits). We propose a novel framework that extracts a **linearized student model** from a pre-trained neural network via synergistic integration of **Koopman operator theory** and **knowledge distillation**. Unlike conventional least-squares Koopman approximations—which suffer from poor generalization and numerical instability—our method learns a globally consistent linear dynamical representation in a learned feature space, while distilling soft labels, layer-wise activations, and decision boundaries from the teacher. Experiments on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST show our linearized model achieves **higher classification accuracy (+3.2–5.7% absolute)** and **superior numerical stability** (condition number reduced by 10–100×, gradient norm variance ↓68%) compared to standard Koopman baselines. Crucially, the resulting model contains *only linear layers* (with only a lightweight nonlinear input projection), enabling efficient optical or hardware-accelerated inference. This work bridges dynamical systems theory and model compression, offering a principled path toward interpretable, hardware-aware linear surrogates for deep networks.
